children.text: Unintelligible speech over ambient street noise
Opening architectural image of a building not named or identified
Establishing shot; static camera

The cart driver bends in prayer while his wife performs domestic labor, pounding or grinding something in a large bowl behind him. Both continue their tasks simultaneously, emphasizing the rhythms and labor of daily life.
Residential area in Dakar’s Medina (domestic courtyard or neighborhood setting)
Static camera; medium-long shot; deep composition that allows both figures to occupy the frame at once; natural lighting

Although the woman's labor occupies the background of the frame, it is essential to Sembène's representation of work in Borom Sarret. As she pounds grain in a large bowl, she performs the everyday labor necessary to sustain the household, yet this work remains unwaged and largely invisible. By framing her labor alongside the man's prayer without privileging one activity over the other, Sembène presents domestic work as an ordinary, continuous part of everyday life rather than an exceptional event. The scene encourages viewers to recognize that labor extends beyond wage-earning occupations to include the routine work that makes daily survival possible.
This sequence can be understood through the lens of Marxist-feminist social reproduction theory, which argues that the household is itself a productive site. As Kirstin Munro explains, "the unwaged productive efforts of household members may include activities such as... preparing food, transmission of knowledge and skills, cleaning one's self and one's environment, and maintenance or making repairs" (Munro 2019, 463). The woman's labor exemplifies this unwaged productive effort. Although she receives no wage, her work transforms raw materials into food that sustains the household and reproduces the labor-power necessary for participation in the broader economy. Rather than depicting domestic labor as separate from economic production, Sembène reveals it to be one of its essential foundations.
Reading this moment through social reproduction theory expands the film's critique of labor beyond the cart driver's struggle for paid employment. Sembène demonstrates that exploitation is not confined to the marketplace but also operates within the household, where reproductive labor is expected yet rarely acknowledged as work. By introducing domestic labor before the narrative follows the cart driver's search for income, the film establishes labor as a broad social relation encompassing both visible wage labor and the hidden reproductive work that sustains workers and their families. This brief shot therefore foreshadows one of Borom Sarret's central concerns: the unequal valuation of different forms of labor in post-independence Senegal.
This sequence can be understood through the lens of Marxist-feminist social reproduction theory, which argues that the household is itself a productive site. As Kirstin Munro explains, "the unwaged productive efforts of household members may include activities such as... preparing food, transmission of knowledge and skills, cleaning one's self and one's environment, and maintenance or making repairs" (Munro 2019, 463). The woman's labor exemplifies this unwaged productive effort. Although she receives no wage, her work transforms raw materials into food that sustains the household and reproduces the labor-power necessary for participation in the broader economy. Rather than depicting domestic labor as separate from economic production, Sembène reveals it to be one of its essential foundations.
Reading this moment through social reproduction theory expands the film's critique of labor beyond the cart driver's struggle for paid employment. Sembène demonstrates that exploitation is not confined to the marketplace but also operates within the household, where reproductive labor is expected yet rarely acknowledged as work. By introducing domestic labor before the narrative follows the cart driver's search for income, the film establishes labor as a broad social relation encompassing both visible wage labor and the hidden reproductive work that sustains workers and their families. This brief shot therefore foreshadows one of Borom Sarret's central concerns: the unequal valuation of different forms of labor in post-independence Senegal.
Pedestrians and cyclists move through the streets of Dakar, passing one another as they navigate the city. The shot captures the steady flow of everyday life.
Street in Dakar’s Medina (urban neighborhood)
Establishing shot; static camera; deep composition, allowing multiple figures to move through the frame simultaneously; natural lighting; observational style

Although no one is identified individually, the shot foregrounds the collective rhythms of urban labor. The pedestrians and cyclists are not presented as anonymous background figures but as participants in the daily circulation of the city. Their movement suggests that labor begins long before work itself; traveling through the city is part of the process of earning a living. By lingering on these ordinary routines, Sembène emphasizes that economic life is constituted through the repetitive, everyday activities of working people rather than through exceptional moments or elite institutions.
The absence of a privileged protagonist in these opening shots is also significant. Rather than centering an individual hero, Sembène first introduces the collective social world in which labor takes place. The city's streets become spaces of movement, exchange, and survival, foreshadowing the cart driver's own dependence on navigating Dakar in search of passengers. This attention to the everyday aligns with Sembène's broader commitment to portraying the lived experiences of Senegal's working class and the structural conditions that shape their lives.

Cars, bicycles, and pedestrians move steadily along a street while the opening title and credits appear over the image. Traffic flows continuously through the frame, emphasizing the rhythm of everyday urban life.
Street in Dakar (urban thoroughfare)
Long shot; static camera; superimposed title and opening credits; deep composition with continuous movement through the frame; observational/documentary aesthetic

The coexistence of multiple modes of transportation is significant. Automobiles, bicycles, and pedestrians occupy the same urban space, reflecting the uneven distribution of resources and mobility within the city. While automobiles symbolize greater economic privilege and access, bicycles and walking represent more affordable means of transportation available to much of the working population. This visual contrast foreshadows the precarious position of the cart driver, whose horse-drawn cart occupies an even more marginalized place within Dakar's transportation economy. By embedding the credits within this dynamic urban environment, Sembène establishes labor, movement, and class inequality as the social context through which the rest of the film should be understood.
The cart driver kneels in prayer, holding a string of prayer beads close to his face. As he prays, his wife continues her domestic labor in the background.
Domestic courtyard/home in Dakar’s Medina
As the cart driver kneels in prayer, he says “Merciful Allah, protect me and mine from the law and infidels! Amen!”

After finishing the prayer, the cart driver lowers his prayer beads, puts on a bracelet, slings a crossbody bag over his shoulder, picks up his hat, and leaves the home to begin his workday. Throughout the sequence, the woman remains in the background performing domestic labor.
As the cart driver finishes his prayer and prepares to leave, string music enters the soundtrack, accompanying the transition from the home to the street.

The cart driver's prayer immediately frames his work as precarious. Rather than asking for prosperity or success, he asks Allah for protection "from the law and infidels," revealing that legal authority is experienced not as a source of security but as a potential threat to his livelihood. Before he has even begun his day's work, the audience learns that survival depends not only on labor itself but also on avoiding the institutions capable of disrupting it. This brief prayer foreshadows the film's later conflict with the police and suggests that the state's relationship to working people is fundamentally coercive rather than protective.
Medium shot; static camera; continuous take with minimal editing; shared composition that includes both the cart driver and his wife; natural lighting

The cart driver’s movement from prayer to preparation frames labor not simply as an act performed in the marketplace, but as something that must be continually reproduced before work even begins. After asking Allah to “protect me and mine from the law and infidels,” he carefully gathers his belongings (bracelet, crossbody bag, and hat) before exiting the home. The string music that enters the soundtrack underscores this transition, not as dramatic elevation, but as a tonal marker of entry into another cycle of precarious work. Sembène slows the moment of departure, emphasizing that labor is not a single event but a repetitive daily process of preparation, survival, and endurance.
From a Marxist perspective, this scene foregrounds the way labor-power must be reproduced before it can be sold. As Marx writes, “The value of labour-power is determined… by the labour-time necessary for the production, and consequently also the reproduction, of this specific article” (Marx 1867, 6). The cart driver cannot simply begin working; his capacity to labor is continuously produced through the domestic sphere: food, rest, care, and the unwaged labor performed by the woman in the household. Her ongoing domestic work is what allows his labor-power to exist each day as something that can be sold in the city.
The scene therefore links domestic space and wage labor as part of a single circuit of reproduction. While the cart driver prepares to enter the streets, the woman remains engaged in sustaining the household that makes his labor possible. Sembène’s composition refuses to isolate wage labor as the only meaningful form of work; instead, it shows that the “moment” of labor in the marketplace is dependent on an entire unseen infrastructure of daily reproduction. The film thus begins to establish labor not as an individual act, but as a continuous social process that moves between home, street, and economy.
The cart driver prepares his horse for work, adjusting its harness and getting it ready for the day. His wife approaches and hands him food. He accepts before leaving for the day.
The cart driver's wife says “Take this. May God be with you! We’ve no food for lunch.” The cart driver accepts it and says “thank you” before he leaves.
Domestic courtyard/home in Dakar’s Medina
Medium shot; largely static camera; natural lighting; camera follows some action, but little movement overall
The cart driver leads his horse through the backyard gate and walks it toward the cart, preparing to attach it and begin his workday.
Transition between domestic courtyard and street-facing work area (threshold of the household).
Medium-long shot; horizontal movement across frame (left-to-right spatial transition); natural lighting

This shot functions as a visual hinge between the domestic sphere and the sphere of wage labor. The cart driver’s movement through the backyard gate marks not just a physical relocation but a shift in the social organization of labor: from reproduction inside the household to circulation in the urban economy. Sembène lingers on this movement, refusing to cut directly to work, thereby emphasizing that labor is produced through transitions and spatial thresholds rather than isolated moments of production.
In Marxist terms, this movement helps clarify how labor-power becomes active labor only after it is socially and materially set into motion. The household produces and sustains the cart driver’s capacity to work, but that capacity must still be carried, literally moved, into the sphere where it can be exchanged for wages. The act of leading the horse toward the cart becomes the final stage in the reproduction of labor-power before it enters circulation.
The gate itself is significant as a spatial marker. It does not simply separate inside and outside; it regulates the passage between unwaged reproductive labor and waged productive labor. The cart, waiting beyond the household threshold, functions as the material embodiment of this transition: it is both the means of labor and the vehicle through which labor-power enters the city. Sembène thus visualizes what is often abstract in Marxist theory: the movement from reproduction to production is not automatic or invisible, but enacted through everyday physical gestures.
By slowing down this passage, the film reinforces its broader claim that labor is not confined to the moment of earning wages. It is a continuous process that begins in the household, passes through thresholds like this gate, and extends into the city’s economic circulation.
In a busy town street, a man walks through the frame and children play nearby. The cart driver enters the scene and crosses horizontally through the urban space as the camera follows his movement.
Street in Dakar (Circulation space)
Medium-long shot; tracking movement (camera follows cart driver); horizontal composition emphasizing lateral movement; deep focus with multiple layers of activity

The village street is shown as the cart driver drives forward in his cart. A young boy suddenly runs out from between houses, jumps onto the back of the moving cart, and then settles beside the cart driver.
Village street/residential urban edge space in Dakar
High-angle establishing shot; static camera; deep spatial composition; emphasis on vertical distance (camera elevated above street level)
The cart driver moves through a busy street filled with many standing and passing people.
Urban street in Dakar (market/crowded pedestrian zone)
Low-angle/ground-level framing; medium tracking shot; emphasis on movement through crowd
The film cuts to a high-angle view of densely packed village houses. The camera slowly pans left across settlement, revealing a tightly clustered residential layout.
Village residential area (dense housing cluster in Dakar)
High-angle establishing shot; slow leftward pan (horizontal sweep); emphasis on architectural density and repetition

The cart driver passes the camera at street level. A woman carrying a basket then appears; he stops and helps her onto the cart. She sits between him and the young boy as they continue driving, while cars pass in the background.
Urban street in Dakar (mixed pedestrian and vehicle circulation zone)
Eye-level shot; static camera positioned in a partially hidden/darkened space; diabetic sound and voice over/internal monologue; minimal camera movement - action passes through frame
The internal monologue of the cart driver is delivered through voice-over: “Fatou’s up early today! When’s she going to pay me? But she has her troubles too!”

This scene introduces a subjective layer to the cart driver’s labor through internal monologue, revealing how economic relations are constantly negotiated within personal and social obligations. His question, “When’s she going to pay me?”, initially frames labor in transactional terms, but this is immediately complicated by his acknowledgment that “she has her troubles too.” This tension highlights the instability of exchange relations in conditions of precarity, where payment is expected but not guaranteed, and where survival often depends on informal networks rather than fixed contracts. The cart driver’s decision to help the woman onto the cart further demonstrates how labor in this economy is not strictly governed by immediate remuneration. Instead, economic exchange is embedded within social recognition and mutual vulnerability. The cart becomes a shared space of circulation rather than a purely commercial vehicle, temporarily reorganizing labor into a collective arrangement shaped by circumstance rather than formal wage relations.

The camera alternates between front and rear tracking shots of the moving cart. The cart driver pulls over as a young boy waits on the street. The boy jumps onto the moving cart. The cart continues through town, with repeated cuts between the horse’s hooves, the cart’s wheel, and the passengers’ faces.
Urban street in Dakar (mixed circulation zone, likely market or transit-heavy area)

Alternating front/rear tracking shots; medium shots with intermittent close-ups; rapid cutting between perspectives (cart, wheel, hooves, faces); insert shots (close up of wheel); emphasis on kinetic movement and continuity; diabetic sound and internal monologue voice-over continue
The internal monologue of the cart driver continues: “And I have to pick up that pest Mamadou! I won’t stop! He ought to stay home! He’s been out of work 6 months but he gets up each morning!”

This sequence intensifies Sembène’s exploration of labor as a process defined by constant movement, interruption, and negotiation. The alternating camera positions (front-facing, rear-tracking, and insert close-ups) fragment the cart’s movement into a series of labor-signifying components. The cart is no longer a stable object but a system in motion: wheels, hooves, passengers, and faces all become discrete visual units of labor in circulation.
The cart driver’s internal monologue reveals the contradictory social logic of his work. His description of Mamadou as “that pest” is immediately undermined by the reality that the boy is part of the same precarious labor economy. The refusal, “I won’t stop!”, is less an assertion of authority than an expression of economic urgency. Stopping means losing time; losing time means losing income. Labor here is structured not by efficiency but by continuous exposure to interruption.
This is further reinforced by the driver’s comment that Mamadou “ought to stay home” despite being unemployed. The irony is central: there is no stable “home” outside labor. The boy’s daily attempt to board the cart reflects the same structural condition the driver himself inhabits: an economy in which survival requires persistent movement even when no work is guaranteed. Labor-power in this world is not activated once but repeatedly tested through daily exposure to uncertainty.

The camera appears positioned on or just above the cart as it moves through the village. Villagers and pedestrians turn their heads to look at the passing cart. There is continuous background movement as people walk, stand, and react to its passage.
Mobile point-of-view style framing (as if from the cart); slightly elevated angle above pedestrians; lateral movement through frame; deep focus with layered crowd activity

The camera shifts to the front and side of the cart as it slows and stops. The young boy gets up and runs off, followed by the other boy who shakes the cart driver’s hand before leaving. Finally, the woman stands, collects her basket, and steps off the cart.
Urban street in Dakar (drop-off point within circulation route)
Alternating frontal and side shots of cart; static camera during stop; medium shots with emphasis on exits from frame; diabetic sound and internal monologue overlay
The cart driver's monologue continues: “All he gives me is a hand-shake! How’ll I feed my horse and family?”

The camera returns to an eye-level perspective from the cart as it moves through the village. The cart passes through busy streets, with villagers continuing to react to its movement. It turns a corner and passes another cart driver with a horse moving in the opposite direction.
Village streets/urban circulation route in Dakar

Eye-level moving shot (cart-mounted perspective); continuous tracking of forward motion; deep focus with layered street activity; naturalistic soundscape and voice-over monologue; lateral movement and turning motion at street corner; brief visual encounter with another cart driver (mirror image)

The cart is stopped. The cart driver lounges sideways inside the cart. A man with a visible disability in his legs and torn clothing crawls into the frame from the direction of the camera and sits in front of the cart. A low-angle shot from the disabled man’s perspective shows the driver’s feet hanging over the cart. A high-angle shot shows only the driver’s feet and the man below. The disabled man pleads: “have pity on me! Have pity on me!” Receiving no response, he crawls away. The camera follows him as he exits.

As the disabled man approaches, the driver’s monologue continues: “For the grace of god!” As the man begs and then begins to crawl away, the monologue continues: “Why answer him? There are so many beggars! They’re like flies!”
Urban street in Dakar (stationary pause within circulation route)

Static frontal shot of stopped cart; low-angle POV shot from ground level (disabled man’s perspective); high-angle shot emphasizing vertical hierarchy (cart above/body below); close observational framing of bodily movement (crawling); tracking shot following exit movement

This scene stages the disabled man’s exclusion from the cart economy as a confrontation with what counts as “productive life.” His crawling entry into the frame and his repeated plea, “have pity on me”, is met with refusal and dismissal, marking him as outside the circuit of circulation that organizes the cart driver’s world. The driver’s response reduces him to a figure of excess: “There are so many beggars! They’re like flies!”
Halberstam’s formulation helps clarify what is at stake in this moment. As she writes, “we might think about failure as a way of refusing to acquiesce to dominant logics of power and discipline and as a form of critique” (Halberstam 2011, 88). In Sembène’s scene, however, failure is unevenly distributed. The disabled man is not able to transform his exclusion into critique; instead, he is rendered as surplus life, excluded from both wage labor and informal circulation. His body exposes the limits of an economic system that defines value through mobility, productivity, and exchangeability.
The cinematography reinforces this hierarchy through alternating high and low angles, placing the cart above and the crawling body below. Labor here is not simply exploitation but a boundary-making process that determines which bodies are legible as economic actors and which are cast out of circulation altogether.

At eye level, the cart driver is still lounging in the cart. A man approaches and calls out, “Wagoner, wake up! We got a job!” He jumps into the cart. The driver repeatedly uses the reins/handles to get the horse moving. The cart finally begins to move and exits the frame while the camera remains static.
Static eye-level framing; diabetic dialogue initiating action; off-screen movement as cart exits frame; fixed camera emphasizing departure of labor from stillness

The cart driver turns a corner and continues driving toward the camera. He is joined by the man seated on the cart. Large barrels of unknown contents are loaded onto the cart, which both men rest on as they ride forward.

Low-angle shot close to street level; frontal approach toward camera (forward motion emphasized); medium-long framing with emphasis on load (barrels and bodies); corner turn introduces spatial reorientation; static camera (cart moves through frame space)

From an eye-level rear shot, the cart driver carries and stacks concrete bricks onto the cart. A cut shifts to a close-up of the horse’s face as the driver pulls the harness, urging it forward. The cart begins to move. The film cuts again to a rear view of the loaded cart as it continues forward, hauling heavy materials through the street.
Urban construction/circulation zone in Dakar

Eye-level rear shot (loading labor emphasized); close up frontal shot of horse (bodily strain); insert emphasis on harness tension (control); rear tracking shot of loaded cart in motion; rapid alternation of perspectives (worker/animal/load)

In darkened lighting, a low-angle shot beneath the cart shows it passing with a creaking, squeaking sound. A man shouts, “wagoner, stop!” The cart halts. Cut to a woman seated, holding her pregnant belly, while her husband says, “Take my wife to the maternity clinic!” The driver responds, “right, boss!” He gets off the cart. He and the husband each support the woman by her arms and help her onto the cart as she appears to be in pain. The sequence ends with a low shot of the horse as the cart moves past, the camera remaining till.

Low-angle shot beneath cart; diabetic squeaking sound emphasizing strain/material wear; sudden interruption of motion (command to stop); medium shots of bodily assistance (lifting/transfer); low angle horse insert shot (static camera, cart passes through frame); strong contrast between movement and forced passage

This scene marks a structural pivot in the film from the circulation of labor and commodities to the urgent demands of social reproduction, specifically pregnancy, care, and medical survival. The interruption of the cart’s movement (“Wagoner, stop!”) breaks the earlier logic of continuous circulation and replaces it with a different temporality: one organized around bodily vulnerability rather than economic flow.
The pregnant woman’s body becomes the center of the scene, not as a productive laboring body in the wage sense, but as a site where reproduction of life itself is visibly at stake. The husband’s request, “Take my wife to the maternity clinic!”, reconfigures the cart from a transport service into an emergency reproductive infrastructure. Labor is temporarily reorganized around care rather than exchange.

In a frontal shot, the pregnant woman rests her head on the cart driver’s shoulder while a squeaking wheel continues. Closer shots show the woman leaning on him and a close-up of the wheel. The cart moves up a circular street, passing a car. A close-up of the wheel follows. Then, from behind, the cart approaches the maternity hospital. The cart stops. The husband and driver help the woman out of the cart, the husband pays the driver, and the husband and wife continue up the stairs. The camera follows them, then cuts to a wider shot of the hospital, and finally a close-up of weeds/flowers on the ground.

As the driver and his passengers (the pregnant woman and her husband) our en route to the maternity hospital, the driver’s internal monologue continues: “That noise again! How’ll I ever fix it! It still squeaks! And her with her head on my shoulder! Why can’t she lean elsewhere? On her husband!” As they approach the hospital, the driver continues “Modern women! I’ll never understand them!” When the hospital is in view, it continues: “Glory to God! We’ve arrived!”
Urban circulation route; maternity hospital exterior (institutional threshold space)

Alternating frontal and rear tracking shots; repeated close ups (wheel/bodily contact); diegetic sound emphasis (persistent squeak); circular street movement (looping spatial structure); passing car (modern transport contrast); rear wide shot revealing hospital as destination; camera follows movement up stairs (vertical transition); final static close-up of ground-level plants (nature detail)

The pregnant woman’s gesture, resting her head on the driver’s shoulder, introduces an intimate form of care that is immediately misread by the driver as irritation and moral commentary. His monologue (“Modern women! I’ll never understand them!”) displaces the actual conditions of exhaustion and pregnancy into a gendered critique, revealing how patriarchal interpretation overlays and distorts the material reality of reproductive labor.
At the same time, the driver is not simply outside care work; he is actively participating in it. Alongside the husband, he physically helps move the woman from cart to hospital steps. This complicates any strict division between productive and reproductive labor. The cart driver is simultaneously a worker in circulation and a participant in social reproduction, temporarily absorbed into a care infrastructure that exceeds wage logic. In this scene, the maternity hospital becomes the institutional endpoint of that reproductive chain, where bodily reproduction is temporarily stabilized through medical intervention. The cart’s labor is thus not separate from reproduction: it is one of the informal infrastructures that makes formal reproductive institutions accessible.
The final sequence of helping the woman into the hospital, payment exchange, camera movement up the stairs, and the cut to weeds on the ground creates a vertical descent from institutional structure back to material remainder. The hospital represents formalized care and state infrastructure; the weeds/flowers return us to neglected, marginal life at ground level. This contrast quietly undercuts the idea that reproduction is fully resolved by institutional arrival.

Villagers wait along the side of a street as the bus passes. The driver’s monologue continues. The bus stops and villagers cross between stalled vehicles. Cut to a medium close-up of the cart driver leaning against the cart, eating kola nuts. Cut back to the street: cars move in one direction while a man walks in the opposite direction.

The driver’s monologue continues on his lunch break: “I won’t get home for lunch. I’ll rest a little. I can’t waste such a good morning. Fatou gave me some kola nuts. That’s enough for my lunch. What’ll they eat at home? Who knows?”
Urban street with mixed circulation (bus route, pedestrian crossing, cart route)

Static observational street framing; mid-close-up of bodily rest/eating; cross-directional movement (cars, bicycles, pedestrians); circulation noise; alternation between public infrastructure and intimate pause

This sequence organizes time around a central contradiction: rest is never fully separate from labor, and food is never fully separate from work. The cart driver’s lunch is not a structured break but an improvised act of consumption: kola nuts given by his wife, eaten while leaning against the cart. What appears as rest is still embedded in circulation; the body pauses, but the system does not. The driver’s statement, “I won’t get home for lunch”, marks a key displacement of the household as a stable site of reproduction. Food, care, and rest are no longer anchored in the home but redistributed across fragmented urban space. Lunch becomes something carried, exchanged, and consumed in transit rather than within a domestic setting.
The cross-directional movement of cars and pedestrians reinforces this fragmentation. There is no single unified direction of urban life; instead, multiple temporalities and velocities coexist within the same frame. The cart driver’s monologue, “What’ll they eat at home? Who knows?”, extends this uncertainty back into the household, underscoring the instability of subsistence itself. If his lunch is reduced to kola nuts, then the household meal is equally uncertain, deferred, or unknown. Ultimately, the scene shows that even rest is circulatory: the cart driver’s pause is temporary, spatially exposed, and materially insufficient.

The cart driver eats while a singer performs nearby. The driver’s monologue shifts to ancestral pride as he watches. The camera pans to reveal a signing man gesturing toward the driver. Villagers gather, smiling and watching. A shoe shiner works in the foreground for another man who leaves without acknowledgment, kicking the platform as he exits. The driver repeatedly pays the singer in stages. The singer and driver eventually shake hands and part ways. The driver then bends down to inspect and attempt to fix the cart wheel.

As the cart driver watches the singer perform, his monologue continues: “Who’s singing about my ancestors? The brave warriors of the past! Their blood flows in my veins. Even if this new life enslaves me, I am still noble like my ancestors!”
Urban street performance zone in Dakar (informal market/circulation zone)

In this sequence, ancestral identity is invoked precisely at the moment of economic exchange. The cart driver’s monologue constructs a narrative of nobility, “the brave warriors of the past”, that attempts to locate dignity outside present conditions of labor exploitation. However, this invocation of ancestral lineage is immediately embedded in a scene structured by payments, performances, and transactional relations. Villagers gather, smile, and watch, yet this collective attention does not dissolve the underlying economic structure. Instead, it produces a moment where cultural expression becomes directly monetized through the driver’s repeated payments. The exchange of money punctures the driver’s monologue of inherited nobility, revealing that even narratives of dignity are embedded within economic relations.
The shoe shiner’s presence intensifies this contrast. His silent labor of bending, polishing, and waiting occurs without recognition or narrative framing. When the client leaves without acknowledgement and even kicks the platform, the scene exposes a stark asymmetry: some forms of labor are rewarded through performance and exchange, while others are structurally invisible, treated as disposable service. This juxtaposition underscores the uneven valuation of labor within the same street economy.

Alternating mid close ups and reaction shots; panning shots revealing layered social space; eye-level shots for exchanges (money, handshake); static close ups for emotional emphasis (smiling faces); background traffic (cars, pedestrians) maintaining circulation layer; final return to mechanical focus (wheel repair)

The cart driver kneels by the wheel, then is approached by a man carrying a deceased infant wrapped in white cloth who says “drive me to the cemetery.” The driver asks “can you pay?” and the man says “Yes.” The infant is placed in the cart while the father follows on foot. Panning shots follow their movement. Close up of the wheel. At the cemetery gate, a clerk demands a certificate and denies entry: “You’ve got no certificate.” The father replies “I’m a stranger here.” The driver attempts to carry the infant inside but is stopped: “Wagoner, stay out!” The infant is left at the father’s feet. The driver returns to the cart and begins to leave. The father’s says “What’s to become of my child?” The driver is shown driving away, but then he stops, gets off, and walks out of frame to pee.
Urban outskirts; cemetery gate

Rear shot of kneeling repair work; medium tracking shots following movement; repeated close-ups of wheel; frontal arrival shot at cemetery gate; static bureaucratic confrontation framing; minimal camera movement at the moment of exclusion (stasis at gate); abrupt tonal shift at end (monologue interruption; exit frame)

As the wagoner leaves the deceased infant and grieving father behind, his monologue continues: “I didn’t want to leave him. But I gave all I had to the story teller.” He says “I have to pee” when he stops the cart before exiting the frame.

This sequence reveals that economic exchange is not sufficient to guarantee access to basic social rites like burial. Even though the father can pay the driver, that payment does not translate into access to the cemetery. Instead, a second regime of circulation appears: one governed by documentation, belonging, and administrative legitimacy. The father’s line, “I’m a stranger here”, is crucial. It marks him as excluded not from economic exchange but from institutional recognition itself. He can participate in market relations (he can pay for transport), but he cannot enter the formal infrastructure of death rites. 
The refusal at the cemetery gate (“wagoner, stay out!”) is especially significant because it marks the boundary between circulation and exclusion. The cart driver, who normally mediates movement across the city, is himself barred from the final destination of movement: burial. His labor is suddenly revealed as partial: he can transport bodies, but he cannot guarantee their social integration or ritual completion.
The father’s final question, “What’s to become of my child?”. remains unanswered, and the film refuses to resolve it. Instead, the narrative shifts back to the cart driver, who abandons the scene entirely. His final line, “I have to pee”, is striking precisely because it collapses the solemnity of death into bodily necessity. It undercuts any sense of narrative closure and returns the viewer to the most basic level of embodied life.

A man in a suit enters the frame and waits for the wagoner to return. He greets him, saying, “Peace be with you! Peace only! I’m moving to the Heights!” The wagoner responds in surprise, “The Heights!” The film cuts to images of the beachfront and the modern district while the wagoner remarks, “Wagoners aren’t allowed up there.” The passenger replies, “I got connections! I pay well.” The wagoner protests, “I never go up there! Not allowed!” The man produces money, saying “Look!” The wagoner reluctantly agrees, rolls up his sleeves, and grabs hold of the horse to begin the journey.
Street near the cemetery; transition toward The Heights, the affluent colonial district of Dakar

Eye-level medium shot of dialogue; cutaway establishing shots of the coastline and modern cityscape; spatial contrast between African quarter and European district; costuming contrast (laborer vs suited passenger); internal monologue conveying hesitation and fear; landscape montage emphasizing geographical separation
As the driver reluctantly agrees and prepares for the journey to the Heights, his monologue continues: “May all the Saints protect me! I’ll chance it.”

This sequence reveals that labor is governed not only by economic necessity but by the colonial organization of urban space. The wagoner immediately recognizes “The Heights” as forbidden territory, responding not with curiosity but with alarm: “Wagoners aren't allowed up there.” His work depends on mobility, yet that mobility is sharply limited by spatial regulations inherited from colonial rule. The city is divided into zones of permitted and prohibited movement, making labor contingent upon access to space.
The suited passenger occupies a different position within this hierarchy. His claim that he has "connections" suggests that access to The Heights is mediated by privilege and social capital rather than equal citizenship. Money becomes a means of persuading the wagoner to assume the risk, but it does not erase the prohibition itself. The wagoner's internal prayer, "May all the saints protect me! I'll chance it!", underscores that crossing into the district is experienced as a dangerous transgression rather than an ordinary job.
Frantz Fanon's analysis of colonial space provides a powerful framework for understanding this moment. Fanon writes, “The colonial world is a world divided into compartments” (Fanon 1963, 38). Sembène visualizes this compartmentalization through the abrupt cut from the crowded streets of the African quarter to the open beaches and orderly architecture of The Heights. The spatial transition is not merely aesthetic; it exposes how colonial power survives through the unequal organization of the city, where movement, work, and belonging remain unevenly distributed after political independence.

The film cuts to a series of establishing shots of The Heights. Orchestral music begins as the camera slowly pans across the modern colonial district. Wide streets, modern buildings, and passing automobiles dominate the frame. Cars travel in multiple directions throughout the city.
The Heights (colonial/European district of Dakar)

Wide establishing shots; slow rightward panning movement; orchestral non-diegetic sound; long takes emphasizing architecture and urban planning; repeated shots of automobile traffic; minimal presence of pedestrians; internal monologue layered over cityscape; strong contrast with the dense, intimate framing of the African quarter

A non-diegetic orchestral score begins as the film transitions into The Heights. The music is composed primarily of sustained strings. It continues underneath the establishing shots of the affluent district while the wagoner’s internal prayers for protection play in voice-over.
As the film displays The Heights, the wagoner’s monologue continues with his prayers for protection: “May all the Saints protect me! May the beloved Sheik protect me! May all protect me!”

The wagoner and the suited passenger ride toward the camera through The Heights. The wagoner’s internal monologue marvels at his surroundings. The film cuts to shots of modern buildings as the monologue continues admiring. A close-up captures the wagoner’s face as he looks from side to side in awe. His attention is interrupted by the cart’s squeaking wheel. The sequence ends with a cut to a point-of-view shot showing children running across the street.
The Heights (colonial district of Dakar)

As the wagoner and the suited passenger make their way through the city, the driver’s monologue continues: “This guy’s lucky to leave the quarter. Look at these streets! That building! It must be nice to live there!” He soon is distracted by his wheel: “That noisy wheel! Saints, protect me!”

Frontal tracking shot of cart approaching camera; architectural establishing shots; close-up reaction shot of the wagoner; point-of-view shot from the driver’s perspective; internal monologue layered over image; continued orchestral score; contrast between expansive streets and intimate close-up

The Heights is defined by wide streets, planned buildings, and orderly movement, while the wagoner's earlier environment was characterized by informal labor, dense neighborhoods, and constant bodily effort. Although political independence has been achieved, the spatial organization of colonial privilege persists.
The close-up of the wagoner's face is especially significant because it humanizes his encounter with this unequal landscape. His curiosity is repeatedly interrupted by the squeaking wheel, a reminder that he cannot simply become part of this world. The cart's deteriorating infrastructure intrudes upon his admiration, symbolizing the material realities of his class position. His renewed prayer, "Saints, protect me!", shows that his awareness of danger has not disappeared despite the district's outward beauty.

The wagoner and the suited passenger ride toward the camera before a police whistle abruptly interrupts them. A close-up shows a police officer blowing the whistle. The cart stops as the horse neighs. The officer approaches, demanding “Let’s see your papers.” The suited passenger quietly exits the cart and leaves the scene. The officer continues, “Have you a permit to come up here? No carts are allowed here!” The wagoner hands over his papers while the officer begins writing a citation. As the papers are unfolded, a medal falls to the ground. The wagoner bends to retrieve it, but the officer deliberately places his foot over it. Looking down, the officer commands, “Beat it! Fast!” The wagoner looks up in confusion, his clenched hand still hovering over the hidden medal. While the officer writes the ticket, the suited passenger enters a nearby automobile without paying. The sequence ends with a low-angle composition of the wagoner beneath the officer, paralleled by a shot from below of a tall building that pans upward.
When the suited passenger enters the car without paying, the driver’s monologue continues: “That man is leaving without paying! This guy doesn’t care! His foot is on my metal!”
The Heights (colonial district of Dakar)

Abrupt interruption through sound (police whistle); close up of cop; alternating close-ups and medium shots during confrontation; high-angle shot emphasizing the wagoner’s vulnerability; symbolic close-up of the fallen medal; blocking through the officer’s foot; low-angle architectural framing emphasizing institutional power

This sequence exposes how colonial power persists not only through spatial segregation but through bureaucratic enforcement and everyday policing. The whistle violently interrupts the wagoner's movement, transforming an ordinary transport job into a disciplinary encounter. His labor is criminalized not because of what he has done, but because of where he has entered. The officer's declaration, "No carts are allowed here!", makes explicit that mobility itself is regulated according to colonial hierarchies.
The most powerful image in the sequence is the officer placing his foot over the fallen medal. Throughout the film, the wagoner's possessions are few, making the medal one of his only visible personal objects. Whether understood as a military decoration, family keepsake, or symbol of honor, the medal represents dignity and personal history. By physically covering it with his boot, the officer does more than prevent the wagoner from retrieving it: he visually subordinates the wagoner's identity to the authority of the colonial state. The wagoner's clenched fist suspended above the officer's shoe captures the tension between resistance and enforced submission.

The wagoner walks beside his horse through the colonial district as automobiles repeatedly pass him. His internal monologue reflects on the events that led to his downfall. Different shots follow him until he reaches a traffic light. The film cuts between his defeated face, modern buildings, city streets, and traffic intersections. The sequence concludes with wide shots of the colonial district’s gates as the wagoner and horse leave The Heights.

As the wagoner and his horse exit the colonial district, his monologue narrates the scene: “This guy left without paying me! I had to hock my cart for the fine. Now what’ll I do? He said he had connections. He’s a thief! How can you trust anyone? It’s the same everywhere. They all know how to read…and lie! It’s his fault. No..the other guy’s fault. The guy whose child died. It was at the cemetery…I got my 2nd customer! And the story teller! It’s his fault. Who cares about my ancestors? Now I’m broke! What’ll I tell my wife? What’ll I give her? Yesterday and the day before it was the same. Red light! Stop! Go! Sleep! It’s a prison! That’s modern life! That’s life in this country! Go! Yes, I’m going! And that house! That building! Yesterday was the same. And the day before! We all work for nothing! What’ll I say at home? What’ll I tell them? How’ll I pay for my cart? I might as well die!”
The Heights; exit gates (prepare for transition back to African quarter)

Montage of multiple walking shots; repeated architectural establishing shots; close-up of driver’s defeated facial expression; POV shots through city streets; wide shots of traffic intersections; repetition of automobiles dominating the frame; symbolic framing of colonial gates; slow editing rhythm emphasizing exhaustion; continuous interior monologue; solemn non-diegetic orchestral score

A slow, solemn orchestral score accompanies the wagoner’s walk after confiscation of his cart. Unlike the earlier orchestral cue that introduced The Heights, this music Is mournful and elegiac, remaining beneath the wagoner’s lengthy internal monologue as he walks his horse through the city.

This sequence reveals the full consequences of the wagoner's encounter with colonial authority. Having lost his cart to pay the fine, he has been separated from the means through which he earned his living. Although he still possesses the capacity to work, he no longer possesses the material instrument necessary to transform labor into income. The cart's absence renders his labor-power economically useless. 
His monologue initially attempts to assign blame: to the passenger who fled without paying, to the grieving father, to the storyteller, but ultimately arrives at a broader realization that "we all work for nothing." This marks an important shift from individual grievance to structural critique. The wagoner recognizes that his suffering is not caused by isolated acts of dishonesty but by an economic and political system that repeatedly extracts labor without guaranteeing security. Karl Marx captures this contradiction when he writes: "The worker becomes all the poorer the more wealth he produces" (Marx 1844, 18). Throughout the film, the wagoner works continuously, transporting passengers, hauling construction materials, carrying the sick, and assisting the grieving, yet each act of labor leaves him no more secure than before. His final dispossession demonstrates that relentless work alone cannot overcome the structural inequalities that govern colonial and capitalist society.

The wagoner walks his horse through the African quarter as villagers pause to look at him. The film cuts to a wide shot of a busy street where automobiles, bicycles, and pedestrians move in both directions. The sequence ends with a panning shot that moves upward to reveal a large monument as a bus passes in front of it.
As the shots of the African quarter take place, the wagoner’s monologue resumes: “This is my neighborhood. I feel good here. It’s not like up there! No policemen here! We all know each other.”
African quarter (return from The Heights)

Medium close-up of the wagoner walking beside his horse; wide establishing shot of neighborhood street life; dense pedestrian movement; slow pan left and upward; monument revealed through camera movement; bus crossing the foreground; continued voice-over narration; naturalistic street sounds

As the wagoner re-enters the African quarter, the solemn orchestral score fades and is replaced with African instrumental music. The music accompanies medium and wide shots of neighborhood streets, villagers, cyclists, and pedestrians while the wagoner’s internal monologue reflects on feeling at home.

The change in music marks the wagoner's return to a familiar social world. Whereas orchestral music accompanied his movement through The Heights underscoring anxiety, surveillance, and exclusion, the African musical score signals a renewed sense of belonging and collective identity. The music works alongside the images of neighborhood life to distinguish the African quarter not simply as a different location, but as a different social environment grounded in shared experience and community.

A wide shot shows the wagoner’s backyard. His wife, carrying their baby on her back, bends over peeling something unidentified into a bowl. Through the open gate, the wagoner enters leading the horse. The camera pans as he returns the horse to its place. The film cuts to the wife glaring at him as he admits, “I don’t have anything for you. Not even a cart!” She stands and silently carries the bowl inside. Two young boys enter the yard. The wife returns, hands the baby to the wagoner, and tells him “I promise you we’ll eat tonight.” She leaves the yard with the boys following ahead of her, telling them, “Go away! There’s nothing here!” The wagoner holds the crying baby as his wife exits out of the gate and the film closes.
Residential area in Dakar’s Medina (domestic courtyard or neighborhood setting)

Wide establishing shot of the household; slow panning camera following the wagoner’s movement; medium shots emphasizing interaction between husband and wife; static framing during moments of silence; close-up of the wagoner holding the infant; minimal dialogue with internal monologue

The film ends where it began: within the household. This circular structure emphasizes that the failures of the day's labor are ultimately borne within the home. Although the wagoner has worked continuously, he returns unable to provide food or income, admitting, "I don't have anything for you. Not even a cart!" His inability to reproduce the conditions of his family's survival immediately shifts responsibility onto his wife, who leaves in search of food while he remains behind caring for the crying infant.
This scene recalls the opening images of the wife performing domestic work. Throughout the film, her labor remains largely unwaged and invisible, yet it is essential to the family's survival. Sembène visualizes this insight by repeatedly showing the wife preparing food and managing the household while the wagoner performs wage labor outside the home. When his labor fails to generate sufficient income, her reproductive labor expands to compensate, illustrating how capitalist crises are absorbed within the household.
The final image of the wagoner holding his crying child reverses the gendered division of labor established at the beginning of the film. He is no longer a worker transporting passengers through the city but a father confronted with the immediate consequences of economic dispossession. The crying infant underscores that labor is never solely about individual wages; it is about sustaining the conditions necessary for family survival. By ending on this image rather than the police encounter, Sembène locates the deepest effects of exploitation not in the workplace but within the intimate sphere of everyday life.
